reference: GATE CS — Probability and Statistics / Statistics

map.condition: Need a single central value for a dataset
Data is skewed or has outliers, need robust center
map.use: \text{Median} = \text{middle value (or average of two middle values)}

map.condition: Comparing variability across datasets with different units
Describing shape of distribution (symmetry / tail weight)

formulas.name: Arithmetic Mean
formulas.whenToUse: Finding the average of a dataset; baseline measure of center.
formulas.trap: Mean is heavily affected by outliers; a single extreme value can shift it significantly.

formulas.name: Weighted Mean
formulas.whenToUse: When observations have different importance (weights), e.g. grade-point average.
formulas.trap: Using simple mean instead of weighted mean when frequencies/weights differ.

formulas.name: Median
formulas.plain: Median = middle value if n is odd; average of two middle values if n is even (data sorted).
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring center for skewed data or data with outliers.
formulas.trap: Forgetting to sort data before finding the middle element.

formulas.name: Mode
formulas.plain: Mode = value that appears most frequently in the dataset.
formulas.whenToUse: Identifying the most common category or value; useful for categorical data.
formulas.trap: A dataset can be bimodal or multimodal; assuming a unique mode leads to errors.

formulas.name: Geometric Mean
formulas.whenToUse: Averaging ratios, growth rates, or multiplicative data (e.g., investment returns).
formulas.trap: Geometric mean is undefined if any value is zero or negative.

formulas.name: Harmonic Mean
formulas.whenToUse: Averaging rates (e.g., speeds over equal distances); always use when unit is 'per something'.
formulas.trap: Using arithmetic mean instead of harmonic mean for rate problems (e.g., average speed) gives wrong results.

formulas.name: AM–GM–HM Inequality
formulas.latex: H \leq G \leq \bar{x} \quad (\text{for positive values; equality iff all } x_i \text{ equal})
formulas.plain: HM <= GM <= AM for positive data; equality holds when all values are equal.
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing different means or bounding one mean using another.
formulas.trap: The inequality reverses if values are negative; it only holds for strictly positive data.

formulas.name: Range
formulas.whenToUse: Quickest measure of spread; useful when data has no extreme outliers.
formulas.trap: Range ignores all intermediate values; one outlier makes it unreliable.

formulas.name: Population Variance
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring average squared deviation from the mean for a complete population.
formulas.trap: Dividing by N-1 (sample formula) when the entire population is given gives a biased (slightly too large) result.

formulas.name: Sample Variance (Unbiased)
formulas.whenToUse: Estimating population variance from a sample; division by n-1 corrects bias.
formulas.trap: Using 1/n instead of 1/(n-1) makes the estimator biased (underestimates population variance).

formulas.name: Standard Deviation
formulas.whenToUse: Expressing spread in the same units as the data (unlike variance which is squared units).
formulas.trap: Standard deviation is not the same as mean absolute deviation; do not confuse the two.

formulas.name: Mean Absolute Deviation (MAD)
formulas.whenToUse: Robust alternative to variance that penalises deviations linearly, not quadratically.
formulas.trap: MAD uses absolute deviations, not squared; it is always <= standard deviation.

formulas.name: Coefficient of Variation (CV)
formulas.whenToUse: Comparing relative variability between datasets with different scales or units.
formulas.trap: CV is meaningless when the mean is zero or near-zero (division by zero).

formulas.name: Interquartile Range (IQR)
formulas.plain: IQR = Q3 - Q1  (difference between 75th and 25th percentile)
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring spread of the middle 50% of data; robust to outliers.
formulas.trap: Outlier fences use 1.5*IQR rule: values below Q1-1.5*IQR or above Q3+1.5*IQR are outliers.

formulas.name: Skewness (Pearson's moment coefficient)
formulas.plain: skewness = mu_3 / sigma^3  where mu_3 is the third central moment
formulas.whenToUse: Quantifying asymmetry: positive = right tail; negative = left tail; 0 = symmetric.
formulas.trap: Positive skewness means the tail is on the right (mean > median > mode), not that most data is on the right.

formulas.name: Kurtosis
formulas.plain: kurtosis = mu_4 / sigma^4;  excess kurtosis = kurtosis - 3 (normal = 0)
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring tail heaviness; excess kurtosis > 0 means heavier tails than normal (leptokurtic).
formulas.trap: Normal distribution has kurtosis = 3 (excess kurtosis = 0); some software reports excess kurtosis as 'kurtosis'.

formulas.name: Empirical Skewness Relation (Pearson)
formulas.whenToUse: Quickly estimating mode from mean and median (or vice versa) for unimodal data.
formulas.trap: This is an empirical approximation, not an exact identity; it fails for multimodal or highly skewed data.

formulas.name: Percentile Location Formula
formulas.plain: L_p = (p / 100) * n   — index of p-th percentile in sorted data of size n
formulas.whenToUse: Finding which sorted position corresponds to the p-th percentile.

terms.means: location index (if non-integer, round up or interpolate)

formulas.trap: Different textbooks use different conventions (round, ceiling, interpolate); GATE typically uses the interpolation convention.

formulas.name: Quartiles Q1, Q2, Q3
formulas.latex: Q_1 = P_{25},\quad Q_2 = P_{50} = \text{Median},\quad Q_3 = P_{75}
formulas.plain: Q1 = 25th percentile, Q2 = median = 50th percentile, Q3 = 75th percentile
formulas.whenToUse: Summarising data distribution with five-number summary (min, Q1, Q2, Q3, max).
formulas.trap: Q2 equals the median only when data are sorted; apply the median formula to the sorted list.

formulas.name: Covariance
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring the direction (not magnitude) of linear relationship between two variables.

terms.means: means of X and Y respectively

formulas.trap: Covariance depends on the scale of X and Y; it cannot be used to compare relationships across different datasets.

formulas.name: Pearson Correlation Coefficient
formulas.plain: r = Cov(X,Y) / (sigma_X * sigma_Y)  ranges from -1 to +1
formulas.whenToUse: Measuring strength and direction of linear association (scale-free).

terms.means: standard deviations of X and Y

formulas.trap: r = 0 means no linear relationship, not no relationship at all (X and Y could still be nonlinearly related).

formulas.name: Variance of a Linear Transformation
formulas.plain: Var(aX + b) = a^2 * Var(X)  — adding a constant does not change variance
formulas.whenToUse: Rescaling data: shifting by b leaves variance unchanged; scaling by a multiplies variance by a^2.
formulas.trap: Confusing SD transformation: SD(aX+b) = |a|*SD(X), not a^2 * SD(X).
